title: Add ACID properties explainer with interactive demonstration
description: ACID is asked in >50% of DB interviews (datainterview.com). We demo isolation levels but never teach Atomicity, Consistency, or Durability. Add an interactive panel showing: (1) Atomicity: bank transfer that partially fails, (2) Consistency: constraint violation rejected, (3) Isolation: already covered, (4) Durability: WAL commit confirmation. Source: CMU 15-445 L17, DDIA Ch7.
acceptanceCriteria: ACID mode accessible from Database module sidebar
Each letter (A, C, I, D) has its own interactive demo with step-through
Isolation links to existing Transaction Isolation mode
Descriptions explain WHY each property matters with real-world examples

title: Add B+ Tree visualization with leaf linked list and range scans
description: SUPERSEDED by DBL-184 (integrate existing BPlusTreeViz). Every real database uses B+ Trees, not B-Trees. CMU 15-445 L8, DDIA Ch3 teach B+ Trees. Our module only has B-Tree (no leaf links, no range queries). Add B+ Tree as a new mode or variant showing: (1) All keys stored in leaves, (2) Leaves linked in a doubly-linked list, (3) Range scan animation traversing leaf chain, (4) Comparison with B-Tree. Interview candidates saying 'B-Tree' instead of 'B+ Tree' is a common mistake.
acceptanceCriteria: B+ Tree mode available alongside existing B-Tree mode
Visualization shows internal nodes (keys only) and leaf nodes (keys + values + next pointers)
Leaf linked list visually connected with arrows
Range scan operation animates traversal along leaf chain
Comparison panel explains B-Tree vs B+ Tree differences

title: Add MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) interactive visualization
description: MVCC is CMU 15-445 Lecture 20, DDIA Ch7, and a top-10 interview topic. Zero coverage in our module. Build: (1) Transaction creates a new version of a row instead of overwriting, (2) Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot based on its start timestamp, (3) Show version chain with xmin/xmax markers (PostgreSQL style), (4) Demo how two concurrent readers see different versions. Source: CMU 15-445 L20, PostgreSQL MVCC docs.
acceptanceCriteria: MVCC mode accessible from sidebar
Visualization shows row versions with transaction IDs (xmin/xmax)
Step-through demo: T1 reads v1, T2 writes v2, T1 still sees v1 (snapshot isolation)
Version chain visualization with garbage collection concept
Comparison with lock-based concurrency control

title: Add CAP Theorem interactive explainer with database classification
description: CAP theorem asked in >50% of system design interviews (training data analysis). We demo consistency levels but never teach CAP. Build: (1) Interactive triangle showing C, A, P tradeoffs, (2) Classify real databases: PostgreSQL=CP, Cassandra=AP, MongoDB=configurable, (3) Demo: network partition happens, show what CP vs AP systems do differently, (4) Explain 'you can't have all 3' with concrete example. Source: DDIA Ch9, Eric Brewer's theorem.
acceptanceCriteria: CAP Theorem mode or section within Distributed/Consistency area
Interactive triangle diagram with C, A, P vertices
Clicking a database name (PostgreSQL, Cassandra, DynamoDB) highlights its position
Partition simulation shows divergent behavior of CP vs AP systems
Links to existing Consistency Level Demo

title: Add SQL vs NoSQL comparison interactive guide
description: SQL vs NoSQL is a top-5 interview question (datainterview.com, Reddit/cscareerquestions). Our module generates both SQL and MongoDB schemas but never teaches WHEN to use which. Build: (1) Comparison table: consistency, scalability, schema flexibility, query power, (2) Decision flowchart: 'Do you need ACID? Do you need flexible schema? Do you need horizontal scaling?', (3) Real-world examples: Instagram=PostgreSQL, WhatsApp=Cassandra, MongoDB for catalogs. Source: DDIA Ch2.
acceptanceCriteria: SQL vs NoSQL comparison section accessible from sidebar
Interactive comparison table with 6+ dimensions
Decision flowchart that recommends SQL or NoSQL based on requirements
At least 5 real-world company examples with reasoning
Links to existing ER→SQL and ER→NoSQL generators

title: Add WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) visualization integrated with LSM-Tree
description: WAL is CMU 15-445 L21, DDIA Ch3, and how every database survives crashes. Our LSM-Tree module shows memtable→SSTable but skips the WAL step entirely. Add: (1) WAL entry created BEFORE memtable write, (2) Show crash scenario: memtable lost, WAL replayed to recover, (3) Checkpoint concept: WAL truncation after flush. Source: CMU L21, PostgreSQL WAL docs.
acceptanceCriteria: LSM-Tree mode shows WAL as the first step before memtable write
WAL entries visible as a log panel alongside the LSM visualization
Crash simulation: 'What if the system crashes here?' — show memtable loss + WAL replay
Checkpoint concept explained: WAL can be truncated after SSTable flush

title: Add Bloom Filter visualization for LSM-Tree read optimization
description: Bloom filters are a key optimization for LSM-Tree reads (DDIA Ch3, Database Internals Ch7). Our LSM read searches all levels sequentially. Add: (1) Bloom filter per SSTable that quickly answers 'is key DEFINITELY NOT in this table?', (2) Show read path: check bloom filter before searching SSTable, (3) False positive demo: bloom filter says 'maybe' but key not found. Source: DDIA Ch3.
acceptanceCriteria: Bloom filter toggle in LSM-Tree mode
Each SSTable shows its bloom filter state (bit array visualization)
Read operation checks bloom filter first, skips SSTable if negative
False positive scenario demonstrated
Read amplification comparison: with vs without bloom filters

title: Add Consistent Hashing visualization with virtual nodes
description: Consistent hashing is asked in most sharding interview questions. Our module has hash/range/directory sharding but no consistent hashing. Build: (1) Hash ring visualization, (2) Add/remove nodes shows minimal key redistribution, (3) Virtual nodes for load balancing, (4) Compare with naive modular hashing. Source: DDIA Ch6, Amazon Dynamo paper.
acceptanceCriteria: Consistent Hashing available as 4th sharding strategy in ShardingSimulator
Hash ring visualization with nodes positioned by hash value
Adding/removing a node shows only affected keys migrating
Virtual nodes toggle showing improved distribution
Comparison metric: keys moved during rebalance (consistent vs naive)

title: Add BCNF detection and decomposition to normalization engine
description: Our normalization engine detects BCNF (normalization.ts:141-154) but decomposeTo3NF only decomposes to 3NF. Universities teach BCNF decomposition as the standard. Add: (1) decomposeToBCNF function using the standard algorithm, (2) Show cases where 3NF decomposition differs from BCNF, (3) Explain the tradeoff: BCNF eliminates all redundancy but may lose dependency preservation. Source: CMU, Silberschatz Ch7.
acceptanceCriteria: decomposeToBCNF function added to normalization.ts
UI shows both 3NF and BCNF decomposition results for comparison
Cases where 3NF and BCNF differ are highlighted
Explanation of dependency preservation tradeoff

title: Add denormalization guide: when and why to break normal forms
description: Production databases are often intentionally denormalized for performance. This is a common interview question: 'When would you denormalize?' Add: (1) Read-heavy vs write-heavy tradeoff, (2) Examples: user profile with embedded address (avoid JOIN), (3) Materialized views as controlled denormalization, (4) Decision framework. Source: DDIA Ch2-3, real-world practice.
acceptanceCriteria: Denormalization section in normalization mode or as a new sub-mode
Interactive example: normalized schema with JOIN cost vs denormalized with redundancy
Decision framework: 'Denormalize when read:write ratio > 10:1'
At least 3 real-world denormalization examples

title: Add Join Algorithms visualization (Nested Loop, Sort-Merge, Hash Join)
description: CMU 15-445 Lecture 12 teaches join algorithms in depth. Our query plan shows 'HashJoin' as a node type but never explains HOW it works. Add: (1) Nested Loop Join animation (O(n*m)), (2) Sort-Merge Join animation (O(n log n + m log m)), (3) Hash Join animation (build phase + probe phase). Source: CMU L12, DDIA Ch3.
acceptanceCriteria: Join Algorithms mode or sub-section in Query Plans
Animated visualization for each of 3 join types with sample data
Step-by-step showing comparisons and matches
Complexity comparison table: when to use which join
Connection to query plan: 'The optimizer chose HashJoin because...'

title: Add Vector Index (HNSW) visualization for AI/ML use cases
description: CMU 15-445 Spring 2026 added 'vector' to its index coverage. DDIA 2nd edition (2025) added vector indexes. pgvector is now standard (Instaclustr 2026 guide). By 2026, vectors are 'a data type, not a database category' (State of Databases 2026). Build: (1) HNSW graph structure with layers, (2) Search animation navigating from top layer to bottom, (3) Comparison with brute-force search, (4) pgvector context. Source: CMU Spring 2026, DDIA 2e, Neon blog.
acceptanceCriteria: Vector Index mode accessible from sidebar as new advanced topic
HNSW graph visualization with hierarchical layers
Insert animation showing multi-layer graph construction
Search animation: greedy traversal from top layer, narrowing at each level
Performance comparison: HNSW vs brute-force on N vectors
Context: 'Used in semantic search, RAG systems, and recommendation engines'

title: Add Row Store vs Column Store comparison visualization
description: Columnar storage is a major 2025-2026 trend (Pavlo review: columnar format explosion, Arrow ADBC standardization). CMU L6 teaches storage models. Add: (1) Same data shown in row-oriented vs column-oriented layout, (2) Show why analytical queries (SUM, AVG on one column) are faster with column stores, (3) Show why OLTP (full row read/write) is faster with row stores, (4) Real systems: PostgreSQL (row), ClickHouse (column), DuckDB (column). Source: CMU L6, DDIA Ch3.
acceptanceCriteria: Row vs Column Store section in storage/indexing area
Side-by-side visualization: same table in row format and column format
Animated query: 'SELECT AVG(price) FROM products' scanning only price column (fast) vs all columns (slow)
Animated query: 'SELECT * FROM products WHERE id=1' reading one row (fast in row store) vs assembling from columns
Real database examples with logos/names

title: Add Database Caching patterns (cache-aside, write-through, invalidation)
description: Database caching is a top interview topic (Reddit/cscareerquestions, MentorCruise FAANG guide). Zero coverage. Build: (1) Cache-aside pattern animation: check cache → miss → read DB → populate cache, (2) Write-through: write cache AND DB simultaneously, (3) Cache invalidation: 'the two hardest problems in CS' — TTL, event-based, manual, (4) Redis as example. Source: System design interviews, DDIA Ch5.
acceptanceCriteria: Caching Patterns mode accessible from sidebar
Interactive animation for cache-aside, write-through, write-behind patterns
Cache hit vs miss visualization with latency comparison
Invalidation strategies comparison: TTL vs event-based vs manual
Interview framing: 'You'll be asked this in system design rounds'

title: Add Write Skew anomaly to transaction isolation demonstrations
description: Our transaction isolation covers dirty read, non-repeatable read, and phantom read. Missing: Write Skew — a subtle anomaly at Repeatable Read level that only Serializable prevents. This trips up senior engineers in interviews. Add: (1) Doctor on-call scenario from DDIA Ch7, (2) Show how two transactions each read OK state and both make conflicting writes, (3) Explain why Repeatable Read doesn't prevent this. Source: DDIA Ch7.
acceptanceCriteria: Write Skew scenario added to transaction-sim.ts
Accessible via a new button or tab in Transaction Isolation mode
Step-through demo with the doctor on-call or similar scenario
Clear explanation of why Repeatable Read allows this but Serializable prevents it

title: Expose existing ER→NoSQL (MongoDB) converter in the UI
description: erToNoSQL function exists at schema-converter.ts:259 and is fully implemented with 1:1, 1:N, M:N handling, composite attributes, and multivalued arrays. But it's not exposed in the DatabaseModule UI — only erToSQL is shown. Add a tab in the SQL output panel showing MongoDB schema output alongside SQL.
acceptanceCriteria: Bottom panel SQL tab gains sub-tabs: 'SQL' and 'MongoDB'
MongoDB tab shows the output of erToNoSQL for the current ER diagram
MongoDB output formatted as JSON schema with field types
Both SQL and MongoDB update when ER diagram changes

title: Add EXPLAIN output reader for PostgreSQL query plans
description: Our QueryPlanSimulation generates plans from heuristics. Working engineers need to READ real PostgreSQL EXPLAIN output. Add: (1) Paste real EXPLAIN ANALYZE output, (2) Parse and visualize as a tree, (3) Highlight expensive nodes, (4) Show actual vs estimated rows, (5) Suggest optimizations. Source: CMU L15-16, use-the-index-luke.com, pgMustard.
acceptanceCriteria: EXPLAIN Reader mode or tab in Query Plans section
Paste box accepts real PostgreSQL EXPLAIN (ANALYZE) output
Parsed into visual tree with cost, rows, time per node
Nodes color-coded by relative cost (green=cheap, red=expensive)
At least 3 sample EXPLAIN outputs to practice with

title: Add difficulty tags and interview relevance markers to all topics
description: No difficulty labels exist on any of the 7 modes. Students can't tell if LSM-Tree is beginner or advanced. Add: (1) Difficulty badge on each mode (Beginner/Intermediate/Advanced), (2) Interview relevance badge ('Asked at Google', 'Common in system design'), (3) Prerequisite hints ('Learn B-Tree before LSM-Tree'). Source: NeetCode patterns, DataLemur categories.
acceptanceCriteria: Every mode in sidebar shows a difficulty badge (Beginner/Intermediate/Advanced)
At least 5 modes show interview relevance tag
Prerequisite hints appear when hovering/clicking a mode that depends on another
Tags are accurate per university curriculum and interview research

title: Add 'real-world applications' section to each existing topic
description: Most topics lack real-world context. For each of the 7 existing modes, add a collapsible 'Where is this used?' section: B-Tree (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB), Hash Index (PostgreSQL hash index, Memcached), LSM-Tree (RocksDB, Cassandra, LevelDB), Normalization (schema design at scale), Query Plans (PostgreSQL EXPLAIN, MySQL EXPLAIN), Sharding (Instagram, Uber), Consistency (DynamoDB, Cassandra).
acceptanceCriteria: Each of 7 modes has a 'Used in Production' collapsible section in properties panel
At least 2 real-world database systems cited per topic with brief explanation
Links to further reading where appropriate

title: Add 'common mistakes' warnings to normalization, indexing, and transactions
description: Students make predictable mistakes: (1) Normalization: confusing 2NF with 3NF, forgetting transitive dependencies, (2) Indexing: adding too many indexes (write penalty), composite index column order matters, (3) Transactions: assuming Repeatable Read prevents all anomalies. Add inline warning callouts at the moments where these mistakes typically occur.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 common mistake callouts in Normalization mode
At least 3 in B-Tree/Hash Index modes
At least 2 in Transaction Isolation mode
Each callout explains what the mistake is and how to avoid it
Callouts styled as warning badges, not intrusive

title: Add SQL interactive sandbox with JOIN/GROUP BY/Window Functions
description: SQL is the #1 most-asked topic in DB interviews (datainterview.com Top 100 SQL Questions 2026, DataLemur 200+ exercises). Our module has ZERO SQL teaching. Build: (1) In-browser SQL executor against sample tables, (2) Guided exercises: SELECT basics, JOINs (INNER/LEFT/SELF), GROUP BY/HAVING, Window Functions (ROW_NUMBER/RANK/LAG), CTEs, (3) Visual result table showing query output, (4) At least 20 progressive exercises. Source: CMU L2, Berkeley CS186 L1-2, DataLemur, pgexercises.com, sqlbolt.com.
acceptanceCriteria: SQL Sandbox mode accessible from Database module sidebar
In-browser SQL execution engine (sql.js/AlaSQL) runs queries against pre-loaded tables
At least 20 exercises covering: SELECT, WHERE, JOIN types, GROUP BY, HAVING, Window Functions, CTEs
Visual results table showing query output with column types
Exercises are progressive: beginner → intermediate → advanced

title: Add index anti-patterns and performance pitfalls guide
description: use-the-index-luke.com devotes entire chapters to index obfuscation — functions on indexed columns (WHERE YEAR(date)=2026 prevents index use), type coercion (comparing varchar to int), LIKE with leading wildcard, low-cardinality columns, over-indexing write penalty. PlanetScale also teaches this. Our module shows indexes working but never shows how they BREAK. Add interactive demos of 5+ anti-patterns. Source: use-the-index-luke.com WHERE clause chapter, PlanetScale MySQL for Developers indexing section.
acceptanceCriteria: Index Anti-patterns section accessible from indexing area or query plans
At least 5 anti-patterns demonstrated: function on column, type coercion, leading wildcard LIKE, low cardinality, over-indexing
Each anti-pattern shows: the bad query, WHY the index isn't used, and the fix
Connected to query plan: show SeqScan caused by anti-pattern, then IndexScan after fix

title: Add Data Warehouse modeling: Star Schema and Snowflake Schema
description: Star/Snowflake schema is asked in data engineering interviews (DataLemur, InterviewQuery) and is standard at every analytics company. Add: (1) Star schema visualization: fact table in center + dimension tables, (2) Snowflake schema: dimensions normalized further, (3) Comparison: query performance, storage, maintenance tradeoffs, (4) OLAP vs OLTP context. Source: DataLemur interview guide, Silberschatz Ch11.
acceptanceCriteria: Star/Snowflake Schema section in Schema Design area
Visual star diagram with central fact table and 4+ dimension tables
Toggle between Star and Snowflake to see normalization differences
OLAP vs OLTP comparison panel explaining when to use each

title: Add Two-Phase Locking (2PL) and Deadlock Detection visualization
description: 2PL is CMU 15-445 Lecture 18 and standard concurrency control. Deadlock detection is a common interview topic. Add: (1) Two transactions acquiring/releasing locks in growing/shrinking phases, (2) Wait-for graph showing deadlock cycle, (3) Deadlock resolution: abort one transaction, (4) Compare with MVCC approach. Source: CMU L18, Silberschatz Ch18, Berkeley CS186 L15.
acceptanceCriteria: 2PL mode within Transaction area showing lock acquire/release phases
Visual lock table showing which transaction holds which lock
Deadlock scenario: two transactions lock resources in opposite order
Wait-for graph with cycle detection highlighted
Comparison panel: 2PL vs MVCC pros/cons

title: Add Distributed Transactions (2PC/3PC) and Saga Pattern visualization
description: 2PC is DDIA Ch9, Petrov Ch13, CMU L23 and asked in senior system design interviews. Saga pattern is the modern microservices alternative. Add: (1) 2PC coordinator-participant animation: prepare→commit/abort, (2) Failure scenarios: coordinator crash, participant crash, (3) 3PC as improvement over 2PC, (4) Saga pattern with compensating transactions. Source: DDIA Ch9, CMU L23.
acceptanceCriteria: Distributed Transactions mode in Distributed area
2PC animation: coordinator sends PREPARE, participants respond, coordinator sends COMMIT/ABORT
Failure scenario: what happens if coordinator crashes after PREPARE?
Saga pattern visualization: forward operations + compensating rollback
Comparison: 2PC vs Saga tradeoffs (consistency vs availability)

title: Add Database Recovery (ARIES) visualization with WAL replay
description: ARIES is CMU 15-445 Lecture 22, Berkeley CS186 L16-18, Silberschatz Ch19. Standard recovery protocol for every major database. Add: (1) ARIES 3-phase animation: Analysis → Redo → Undo, (2) Show dirty page table and transaction table during analysis, (3) Redo all logged operations from checkpoint, (4) Undo uncommitted transactions, (5) Crash scenario showing data loss without recovery vs successful recovery with ARIES. Source: CMU L22, Silberschatz Ch19.
acceptanceCriteria: ARIES Recovery mode accessible from sidebar under Transactions/Storage area
3-phase animation: Analysis scans WAL, Redo replays committed ops, Undo rolls back uncommitted
Crash simulation: inject crash at arbitrary point, show recovery process
Dirty page table and transaction table visualized during analysis phase
Checkpoint concept explained and shown in WAL timeline

title: Add Buffer Pool Manager visualization with page eviction policies
description: Buffer pool is CMU 15-445 Lecture 4, Berkeley CS186 L3+L8. Every database caches disk pages in memory — understanding this is crucial for performance tuning. Add: (1) Page table mapping page IDs to buffer frames, (2) Page request: check buffer → hit or miss → fetch from disk, (3) Eviction policies: LRU, Clock, LRU-K comparison, (4) Dirty page flush on eviction, (5) Pin count mechanism. Source: CMU L4, Berkeley L8.
acceptanceCriteria: Buffer Pool mode showing fixed-size array of buffer frames
Page request animation: check page table → buffer hit (green) or miss (red, fetch from disk)
Toggle between LRU, Clock, and LRU-K eviction policies
Dirty pages marked, flush animation on eviction
Hit rate counter showing buffer efficiency

title: Add Connection Pooling patterns visualization
description: Connection pooling is a top production concern (PlanetScale teaches it, Prisma Data Guide covers it, Reddit mentions it as frequent interview topic). Uber's CacheFront handles 40M+ reads/sec partly through connection management. Add: (1) Without pooling: each request opens/closes TCP + auth, (2) With pooling: pre-warmed connections reused, (3) Pool sizing: too small = queue, too large = resource exhaustion, (4) PgBouncer/HikariCP patterns. Source: Prisma Data Guide, production engineering guides.
acceptanceCriteria: Connection Pooling section in a Production Patterns area
Animation: request lifecycle without pooling (slow: connect, auth, query, disconnect) vs with pooling (fast: borrow, query, return)
Pool size simulator: slider for pool size, show queue depth and latency
PgBouncer modes explained: session, transaction, statement

title: Add covering indexes and index-only scan demonstration
description: use-the-index-luke.com and PlanetScale both teach covering indexes as a key optimization. A covering index includes ALL columns needed by a query, eliminating the need to read the actual table row (index-only scan). Our module shows indexes but never this optimization. Add: (1) Query that needs 2 columns, (2) Non-covering index: index lookup → then heap fetch (2 reads), (3) Covering index: index lookup only (1 read), (4) INCLUDE clause for covering indexes. Source: use-the-index-luke.com clustering chapter, PlanetScale covering indexes video.
acceptanceCriteria: Covering Index demo accessible from B-Tree or Query Plans mode
Side-by-side: non-covering (index + heap fetch) vs covering (index only)
Visual showing the extra I/O hop eliminated by covering index
INCLUDE clause syntax shown for PostgreSQL and MySQL

title: Add cursor/keyset pagination vs offset pagination comparison
description: Offset pagination breaks at scale (OFFSET 10000 still scans 10000 rows). PlanetScale and use-the-index-luke both dedicate sections to cursor/seek pagination. This is a common production issue and interview topic. Add: (1) Offset pagination demo: show growing cost as OFFSET increases, (2) Cursor/keyset pagination: WHERE id > last_seen_id LIMIT 20, (3) Visual comparison of I/O cost, (4) When to use each. Source: use-the-index-luke.com pagination chapter, PlanetScale cursor pagination video.
acceptanceCriteria: Pagination section in Query Plans or Production Patterns area
Offset demo: slider for page number, show increasing query cost
Cursor demo: same pages but constant query cost
Side-by-side cost comparison graph: offset grows linearly, cursor is constant
Code examples for both patterns

title: Add visual JOIN explainer with Venn diagrams and animated row matching
description: JOINs are the #1 thing students struggle with (LearnSQL.com, Frontiers in Education 2025 study). 75% of students fail complex joins. Add: (1) Venn diagram for each JOIN type (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS), (2) Animated row-by-row matching showing which rows survive each JOIN, (3) SELF join with a practical example, (4) Anti-join pattern (LEFT JOIN WHERE right IS NULL). Source: LearnSQL, DataLemur anti-join guide, sqlbolt.com JOINs lesson.
acceptanceCriteria: JOIN Explainer accessible from SQL area
Venn diagram for each of 5 JOIN types with highlighted matching areas
Two sample tables with 5-6 rows each, animated row matching
Result table builds row by row as matches are found
Anti-join pattern shown: LEFT JOIN + WHERE IS NULL
SELF join with employee-manager example

title: Implement 6 learning paths with progress tracking
description: The content audit designed 6 learning paths: (1) University CS Student 12-week, (2) FAANG Interview Prep 4-week, (3) Senior Engineer Upskill 2-week, (4) Competitive Programming 8-week, (5) Weekend Crash Course 2-day, (6) AI/ML Engineer (2026). Each path is a sequence of topics from the module. Implement: path selector, topic checklist per path, progress bar, 'next topic' suggestions. Source: curriculum design from content audit.
acceptanceCriteria: Learning Paths section accessible from module header or sidebar
6 paths selectable with description and estimated time
Each path shows ordered topic list with checkboxes for completion
Progress bar showing percentage complete per path
Next topic suggestion based on current progress
Progress persisted to localStorage

title: Add prerequisite graph showing topic dependencies
description: Users don't know what to learn before what. Build a visual prerequisite graph: ER Diagrams → Normalization → SQL Generation, B-Tree → B+ Tree → LSM-Tree, ACID → Isolation Levels → MVCC → 2PL, SQL Basics → JOINs → Window Functions, Sharding → Consistent Hashing, etc. Clickable nodes navigate to the topic. title: Add interview tips section to each topic with company-specific context
description: Each topic should have an 'Interview Tips' collapsible section: B-Tree → 'Google asks: explain how a B+ Tree handles range queries', MVCC → 'Asked at Apple/infra teams: how does PostgreSQL handle concurrent reads?', Sharding → 'Amazon: design the shard key for a user table'. title: Add quiz/self-test after each visualization mode
description: Active recall dramatically improves learning (Roediger & Karpicke 2006). After completing a visualization (e.g., B-Tree insert), show 3 quiz questions testing understanding: 'How many comparisons does a search through a 3-level B+ Tree need?', 'What triggers a node split?', 'What is the worst-case time complexity?'. Source: VisuAlgo quiz system (proven model), Brilliant.org quiz-per-lesson pattern.
acceptanceCriteria: Quiz button appears after user has interacted with a mode for 60+ seconds

Instant feedback: correct answer highlighted green, wrong shows explanation
Score tracked per session (e.g., '4/5 correct on B-Tree Quiz')
Questions are randomized from a pool to allow retaking

title: Add topic history/story for B-Tree, LSM-Tree, and normalization
description: Every great explanation starts with WHY the concept was invented. Add brief history panels: B-Tree (Rudolf Bayer, 1970, needed balanced tree for disk access), LSM-Tree (Patrick O'Neil, 1996, needed to optimize write-heavy workloads for log-structured systems), Normalization (Edgar Codd, 1970, Third Manifesto, eliminating data redundancy). 3Blue1Brown style: the STORY makes the concept memorable. Source: Wikipedia history sections, original papers.
acceptanceCriteria: History/story collapsible section in Properties panel for B-Tree, LSM-Tree, Normalization
Each story: who invented it, when, what problem they were solving, why it matters today
Written in conversational tone, not academic
At least 3 topics have history sections (expandable to all topics later)

title: Add database migration strategies guide (expand/contract, zero-downtime)
description: Database migrations are a weekly production concern (Prisma Data Guide covers expand/contract pattern, PlanetScale teaches online schema changes). Missing from our module. Add: (1) Expand/contract pattern: add new column → backfill → switch reads → drop old column, (2) Zero-downtime migration steps, (3) Common pitfalls: locking tables, backfill timeouts, rollback strategies, (4) Tool context: Prisma migrate, Flyway, Liquibase. Source: Prisma Data Guide migrations section.
acceptanceCriteria: Migration Strategies section in Schema Design or Production Patterns area
Animated expand/contract pattern showing 4 steps
Zero-downtime checklist with do's and don'ts
At least 3 common migration pitfalls explained

title: Add slow query identification and analysis guide
description: Slow query analysis is a daily production skill (PlanetScale teaches slow query logs, pgMustard built a business around this). Our query plan module shows theoretical costs but doesn't teach real-world query debugging. Add: (1) What a slow query log entry looks like (PostgreSQL pg_stat_statements, MySQL slow_query_log), (2) Key metrics: execution time, rows examined, rows returned, (3) Decision tree: is it missing an index? Bad join? Table scan? Lock contention? Source: PlanetScale MySQL for Developers, PostgreSQL pg_stat_statements docs.
acceptanceCriteria: Slow Query Analysis section in Query Plans area
Sample slow query log entries with annotated explanations
Decision tree: 'Is rows_examined >> rows_returned? → Likely missing index'
Connected to existing query plan visualization: 'This slow query produces THIS plan'

title: Add serverless database architecture explainer (Neon, PlanetScale, Aurora)
description: Serverless databases are a major 2025-2026 architectural shift. Databricks bought Neon for $1B (Pavlo 2025 retrospective). Scale-to-zero, copy-on-write branching, and separation of storage/compute are reshaping how databases are deployed. Add: (1) Traditional vs serverless architecture comparison, (2) Storage-compute separation diagram, (3) Scale-to-zero concept, (4) Database branching for dev/preview environments. Source: Pavlo 2025 retrospective, Neon docs, PlanetScale docs.
acceptanceCriteria: Serverless DB section in Distributed/Modern area
Traditional (monolithic) vs serverless (disaggregated) architecture diagram
Scale-to-zero animation: compute spins down, storage persists, compute restarts on query
Branching demo: create a branch, make changes, merge back
Real-world context: Neon, PlanetScale, Aurora Serverless v2
